videos.transcript: Habibi come to Dubai hey guys we're Ian and Anna and we just spent the last two weeks in the incredible futuristic city of Dubai in this video we want to share with you our top 15 favorite things to do in this city let's start off with number 15 Miracle Garden this three-time Guinness World Record breaker is filled with 150 million flowers and is the perfect place for not only flower lovers but also children you'll notice just how family friendly it is when you initially walk in and are greeted by two Immaculate shrub stallions that serve as a gate to the rest of the Majestic Garden there's endless fun things to see including a Smurf Village a flower princess floating above her castle and the world's biggest floral installation which is that life-sized replica of the Emirates airplane as I'm sure you know Dubai gets pretty hot so we recommend coming as soon as it opens at 9am you only need to spend one to two hours to see everything you want and if you have some extra time you can head over to the world's largest butterfly garden that holds 15 000 butterflies coming up is number 14 Dubai Mall the Dubai Mall is the largest mall in the world and is roughly the size of 200 football fields it has more than 1200 stores with every brand you could possibly think of in around 200 restaurants you could definitely open entire day here especially because it's connected to the Burj Khalifa and right outside its doors is the famous water show other mind-blowing things you could find here is an Olympic-sized ice skating rink the world's largest indoor aquarium and even 150 million year old dinosaur which was discovered in Wyoming if you're not a fan of crowds avoid this area at all costs because it gets around 75 million visitors a year and can feel quite claustrophobic next up is number 14 the iconic Burj Khalifa since 2009 the Burj Khalifa has been the tallest building in the world at 828 meters high it's equivalent to the size of three Eiffel Towers or two empire state buildings it cost a whopping 1.5 billion dollars to complete this structure there's 160 floors and you can ride all the way to 154 to visit the highest outdoor observation deck in the world don't worry there's glass surrounding it so you won't fall off I don't think people realize just how big this building is if you're standing at its base you have to crank your head all the way back just to see the tip of it and why you turn it sideways so they can see like how it doesn't even fit in the frame oh my God the building's exterior contains 26 000 glass panels and I know what you're thinking how do they keep it so clean while they hire 120 glass cleaners and it takes them three months to clean the entire building and of course while you're there just outside the Burj Khalifa you can see the world's largest water show the Dubai Fountain was designed by a california-based company also responsible for the creation of the Bellagio Fountains in Las Vegas it was so cool taking in the water show from above on the observation deck and then seeing it again from the ground floor The Fountains dance every 30 minutes starting at 6 PM the bird lights up in the background and is truly a sight to see apparently this spot is also very cool at New Year's when there are fireworks shooting from the building combined with a truly Sensational water show next we have number 12 Museum of the future this has to be one of the coolest buildings we've ever seen and it makes sense because its nickname is the most beautiful building on earth the symbolism behind it though makes it even more impressive the building's circular form represents Humanity the green Mound it sits on represents the earth and the void in the middle represents our unknown future wrapping the outside of the windows are three quotes from Sheikh Mohammed who is the prime minister of the UAE and the ruler of Dubai and these quotes in Arabic are his thoughts and vision for the city's future the Museum of the future officially opened to the public in 2022 so it's brand new and that's why we couldn't even get a ticket to enter we highly recommend you book online before arriving so we'll make sure to put a link in the description below inside you can expect to see the future of space travel robots Innovation and sustainability even if you can't enter the museum you can still check out the lobby which looks like you're walking into the future it's an all-white room with spiral staircases mesmerizing elevators and a ceiling that glows up with more Arabic quotes personally the best time to visit is at Sunset and when it gets dark this entire structure glows in the night with the city shining all around it up next is number 11 The Unbelievable Palm Jumeirah there are three man-made islands off the coast of Dubai but the biggest and most famous is the Palm Jumeirah this palm trees shaped island stretches five kilometers into the Arabian Gulf and it's so massive that you can see it from space to create this they blasted 7 million tons of rock from a nearby mountain and dug up sand below the Persian Gulf it costs 12 billion dollars and took six years to build the famous Atlantis stands at the front of the palm and here you can have a full day of fun at the water park which broke the world record for most water slides we actually did spend a day here and it was an absolute blast I think if you have kids this is the perfect spot to get some of their energy out definitely visit the Tower of Neptune first to go on the leap of faith Atlantis most famous water slide if you're an adrenaline junkie and want to go skydiving I don't know of many other places that you can skydive with a view like this one this is actually the only way to see the Palm from above other than taking a helicopter ride let us know in the comments below if you've gone skydiving in Dubai because we would love to hear your opinion let's move on to number 10. rooftop bars and pools Dubai is all about that go big or go home mentality and they definitely didn't skimp out on rooftop pools and bars of course Dubai holds two more records first it has the world's highest infinity pool and second it has the world's highest 360 degree infinity pool called Aura sky pool the one we really wanted to visit was the aura sky pool but of course it was booked out for two weeks when we were there so please make sure to book reservations far in advance if you like to drink alcohol with city views we have heard great things about level 43 Sky Lounge which is located on the roof of the Sheraton Hotel this Sky Lounge overlooks Sheikh Zaid Highway which is this famous road that connects all seven Emirates in the UAE it has an astounding 14 lanes and during your time in Dubai you will definitely spot supercars flying down this highway now on to number nine the Dubai frame so be completely honest with you guys at first I thought the concept of a gold-plated giant picture frame in the middle of the city was a little odd I kind of didn't want to go but then I found out what it symbolized the Dubai frame represents a connection between both the past and the present in this rapidly changing City when you're at the top of the building on one side you'll see old Dubai where everything began and on your other side you're going to be seeing the most futuristic city in the world that has just been built in the last 40 years time when you walk outside that elevator the floors are actually censored so when you step on them they turn clear and you can see all the way to the ground below you even gave us a pretty good Adrenaline Rush stepping on those floors coming up we have number eight Dune buggying a trip to Dubai is not complete without visiting the sand dunes surrounding the city we went on a safari tour that we'll talk about later in the video but right now is all about dune buggy we ended up choosing the cheapest one hour tour that we could find which was 156 dollars total for two people that means each driver will get around 30 minutes behind the wheel which was more than enough time for us for our tour we decided to get up bright and early one morning at 7 A.M and head one hour to the sand dunes which was the best decision not only is it less hot but we had the entire tour to ourselves because no one wanted to get up that early just so you know in Dubai it's a law that you need a tour guide to lead you around the sand dunes and at first I was super disappointed especially because it started out really slow basically at the beginning the guide wants to feel out your driving skills and once you are more confident he definitely lets you go much faster just in case the tour companies run out of helmets with face masks make sure to bring sunglasses so the sand doesn't spray in your face the whole time whizzing up and down the sand dunes is not only thrilling but hilarious because as the passenger you never really know what your partner is going to do there are endless kinds of tour companies for everyone's budget and with different high-speed dune buggies I'll make sure to link a couple below for you guys next up is number seven Hell's Kitchen now if you haven't seen the short we posted on this you should go watch it ASAP you'll really understand my love for all things Gordon Ramsay if you don't know what house kitchen is it's a restaurant inspired from Gordon Ramsay's famous TV show Hell's Kitchen we've had the pleasure of eating at HK at Caesar's Palace in Vegas and it was so good any chance we get to eat at it again we take the opportunity oddly enough this is his only Hell's Kitchen restaurant outside of the US everything is designed just like the TV show where there's a red and blue team and they cook right in front of you our highly recommended dishes to you would be the brata salad followed by Gordon's famous Beef Wellington which is so freaking delicious you have to get it best dessert we've ever had in our life the famous sticky toffee pudding oh my mouth is watering just thinking about it moving on to number six food tour let's be honest food is the best part of travel and that's why in every place we visit around the world we always start our time with a local food tour you not only get to try some of the best food during your trip but you also get to make friends with locals that will teach you all about the culture we met our tour guide Sandy in Dara which is the heart of Dubai's Old Town here we stroll through the Maze of narrow Alleyways passing by classic Sandstone buildings and trying all sorts of dishes like camel gelato Falafel pitas chicken shawarma and much more then it was time to pass through the golden spice souks where there are mounds of colorful spices and more gold than we've seen in our lives lastly we crossed the Dubai Creek on traditional boats to visit burdubai and have delicious messy platters and some fresh squeezed orange juice throughout our experience Sandy was begging us to try the camel Burger but Anna had zero interest because that's her favorite animal of course I'm willing to try a bite of just about anything so Sandy brought us the most famous spot for Camel bear to be honest I felt guilty the entire time because in this restaurant you are surrounded by tons of cute camel artwork and wallpaper visiting the old town of Dubai was such a breath of fresh air especially when you're surrounded by skyscrapers all day now let's move on to number five jet skiing although you can jet ski anywhere in the world there are very few places that offer as epic of a Skyline as jet skiing in Dubai does within one hour we were able to see the Burj al-arab the Atlantis and the mass of Dubai Skyline with a Burj Khalifa in it we booked a one-hour session in the morning and let me tell you I don't think it was enough we recommend going in the morning at 9am not only is it a great way to wake up but the waves are the calmest at that time even though Sunset sounds like it would be nice and fun the water gets super choppy and it's way more crowded which allows you less room to go wild either way you can't get too wild because it's a requirement in Dubai to jet ski with a tour guide but our guy was super chill and Ian reached 70 miles per hour on his jet ski so they're not extremely restrictive quick announcement this entire trip and video would not be possible without our sponsor get your guide get your guide is a platform with over 65 000 experiences worldwide everything you've seen this video was booked through their app they offer tour guides experiences entrance fee tickets and even skip the line opportunities just like the Burj Khalifa We got to skip the entire massive line there and they also let you cancel 24 hours before your tour starts and you get all your money back everything will be linked down in the description below and we get a small little Kickback so we can keep creating travel guys just like this one next up number four Dubai Marina and beaches Dubai is probably one of the most well-rounded cities in the world you have world record breakers for pretty much any category beautiful architecture old and new deserts and more fun than you could feel in a lifetime but to have all this and be right next to the Persian Gulf with amazing beaches you really can't beat it now when visiting Dubai you'll probably stay in one of two locations either in the city center close to the Burj Khalifa or 25 minutes down the road near Dubai Marina during our two weeks in Dubai we split up our time and stayed seven days downtown and seven days in the Dubai Marina and honestly if we can go back I would have spent more time near the marina there's just nothing like having a hundred dollar Airbnb a five minute walk from the beach there are plenty of beaches lining up the coast but we visited the most popular called jbr Beach right near the marina even though it's super crowded it was the best spot to take in the sunset watch Yachts Roll by and even see the largest ferris wheel in the world which was inspired by the London Eye another fun thing you could do at the marina is take dinner cruises up and down the coast at night which would be such a relaxing way to see different perspectives of Dubai next up is number three Deep dive Dubai Deep dive Dubai is another world record-breaking facility allowing Thrill Seekers to dive into another dimension this is the deepest pool in the world at 60 meters deep but it's so much more than a pool it's basically like exploring a sunken city it seems like there are so many rooms and crevices filled with unique things to do there's even a game room where you can play foosball or chess that is like all my favorite things combined in one place deep dive Dubai is the one thing I really really wanted to do unfortunately it's a bit expensive and it was all booked up while we were there all the more reason to visit again though next up number two Abu Dhabi even though this one isn't located in Dubai and Abu Dhabi day trip is a must do Abu Dhabi is the largest Emirate and also the capital city of the UAE if Dubai is the financial and tourism Hub then Abu Dhabi is the oil capital and that's because 96 of the country's oil is found in this one Emirate right now to visit Abu Dhabi you need a kova test but most tour companies will help you set one up at your hotel beforehand after driving an hour and a half from Dubai to Abu Dhabi you can first stop at the Louvre Museum if you haven't noticed Dubai likes to copycat a lot of famous landmarks around the world and they went over the top for the Louvre just for the name itself the UAE paid France 500 million dollars for a 30-year contract although this museum has a bunch of cool art it's nothing compared to the galleries in Paris but the real thing to be excited about here is this spectacular architecture this massive floating Dome on top of an all-white building contrast perfectly with the surrounding blue water what's really cool about the Dome is that it has eight different layers which creates this rain of light effect coming through as you're walking around after Lube you can visit many more places like the Emirates Palace which is now turned into a hotel that you could stay at and then you could hop right over to the presidential Palace where all the government meetings are held now one of the main reasons you're probably wanting to visit Abu Dhabi is for the Grand Mosque which is the ninth largest mosque in the world and can fit upwards of 40 000 worshipers at once this was by far the prettiest Moss we've ever been to especially because it's all white marble walking around I couldn't believe my eyes because it was just perfection keep in mind there is a dress code here where men have to cover their shoulders and knees and women have to cover pretty much everything besides their face thankfully our tour guide was nice enough to bring an extra Abaya which is a traditional outfit of amarati women another thing to keep in mind is that this is a place of worship so please be respectful the security guards do not want you touching each other in photos giving peace signs or basically any movement other than smiling now coming in at number one 1. the Safari tour a safari tour is something everyone should experience at least once in their lifetime when you leave the city and approach the desert you really get to see what a local Bedouin lived like Bedouins are nomadic Arab tribes who have historically inhibited the desert regions although there aren't many Native Bedouins left in the UAE their Traditions are passed down and celebrated with visitors if you have a layover or just a short amount of time in Dubai you should definitely book a desert Safari tour here's everything that's included first you'll get picked up from your accommodation it's about an hour drive to the desert and the first thing offered is camel riding although we skipped with camel riding we did get to say hi to some baby camels after all they are my favorite land animal next we rode in a d-class vintage Mercedes throughout the sand dunes which was so much fun it was a total Vibe especially once the most beautiful sun began setting along the ride we spotted wild gazelles and even the incredible Arabian oryx the Arabian oryx were so cool I can't even describe private these are the official animal of the UAE and they're extremely long straight horns will truly blow you away when you're done adventuring you'll head back to camp for a delicious feast including all of the best Middle Eastern foods like hummus taboo Leaf fattoush lamb and finally you'll end the night with some traditional dancing and fire shows I absolutely love nights like these and I can't recommend doing a desert Safari tour enough and that is a wrap make sure to like and comment on this video so it gets pushed out to Travelers just like you we actually have two more videos you could watch on Dubai in this playlist right here right now we are in Bali for the next month and a half and we're gonna have tons of videos on that so make sure to subscribe so you always get notified foreign

videos.transcript: Dubai is dazzling. Futuristic skylines, desert landscapes, and world class luxury at every turn. But behind the glamour are rules, expectations, and cultural norms that can catch visitors off guard. In 2025, this city is evolving fast. And if you arrive unprepared, you might face fines, misunderstandings, or miss out on what makes Dubai truly special. From local laws to travel traps, even simple things like what you wear or how you pay can make a big difference. In this video, I'm breaking down 15 essential things to know before visiting Dubai, including hidden rules, smart tips, and common mistakes travelers often regret. If Dubai is on your list this year, this is the video to watch before you go. Even before your plane descends over Dubai's iconic coastline, make sure you're even allowed to enter. If you're from The US, Canada, The UK, most of Europe, or Australia, Good news. You get visa free entry on arrival for up to thirty or ninety days. No paperwork, no pre approval. Just make sure your passport is valid for at least six months from the date you land. But don't get comfortable. Overstaying even by a day can lead to hefty fines or a ban from reentering. If your country isn't on the visa free list, you will need to apply online or through your airline, and that can take several days. Don't leave it to chance. It's one of the few things in Dubai that isn't high-tech but if you get this wrong nothing else matters. Speaking of things going wrong, let's talk about the traps that start showing up the moment you land. Dubai is polished and safe, but that doesn't mean it's scam free. The most common traps are subtle, not shady, like unlicensed taxis waiting outside malls and airports. They smile, they nod, then charge you three times the regular fare. Always use official RTA taxis or book through Careem or Uber. Then there are the markets. The Gold and Spice Souks are iconic, but vendors often quote inflated prices to tourists who don't know how to bargain. If you pay the first price, you're paying way too much. Some free tours stack on hidden costs. Snacks, tips, surprise entrance fees, and fake luxury goods, they look real until they break two days later. Dubai won't pick your pocket, but if you're not paying attention, it might gently empty your wallet. Now, let's talk about another thing that trips up visitors even before they leave the hotel. Let's clear up one of the most misunderstood parts of visiting Dubai. Yes, you can wear shorts. Yes, you can wear tank tops. But context is everything. At the beach or by the pool, swimwear is totally fine. But the moment you step into a mall, metro or mosque, you'll need to cover your shoulders and knees. In fact, Dubai Mall even displays signs reminding visitors to dress modestly. For women, no need to wear a hijab unless you're entering a mosque. For men, avoid walking around shirtless anywhere outside the beach. You likely won't get in trouble for dressing casually, but you will stand out. And in more conservative areas you could be denied entry or even warned by security. Dubai isn't strict like some neighboring regions, but it does expect respectful attire in public. But clothing isn't the only thing that could get you flagged, your phone might too. What you post in Dubai can get you into serious trouble even if it seems harmless back home. Taking photos or videos of people without their permission, especially women or government workers is a big no. Sharing them online? That could lead to fines, detention or deportation. You also can't film inside airports, malls or some government buildings, even with your phone. And avoid posting anything that could be seen as mocking local culture or religion, even as a joke. Dubai has strict cybercrime laws and they apply to tourists just as much as locals. So before you hit record, ask yourself, would I want this to go viral for the wrong reasons? If that sounded strict, wait until you see how easily your wallet can take a hit. Dubai loves luxury, but it doesn't always love transparency. Many restaurants and hotels add service charges, tourism fees, or taxes that aren't included on the menu. A meal listed at $25 might end up costing $35 after everything is tacked on. Taxis? The meter might start at one rate and spike with tolls or waiting charges you didn't anticipate. Hotel extras like breakfast, early check-in or even wifi and budget rooms might cost more than you'd expect. And then there are the high end experiences. Rooftop lounges, infinity pools, beach clubs, they look free to enter until you realize there's a minimum spend of $100 just to sit down. Dubai doesn't try to scam you. But it's easy to overspend if you don't read the fine print. But here's the good news. Some of the best experiences in Dubai don't cost a Durham. Not every skyline shot has to come with a $50 price tag. Dubai has some incredible free or low cost viewpoints that tourists often miss. Wanna see the entire skyline without paying for a rooftop? Try Dubai Creek Harbour at Golden Hour or the Palm Jumeirah Boardwalk for sweeping views of Dubai Marina. And for something even more immersive, walk right through Dubai Marina itself. Prefer something iconic? Just near the Museum of the Future, you'll find walkways and pedestrian bridges offering stunning views day or night. And if you want something truly cinematic, head to the promenade outside Dubai Mall in the evening. The Dubai Fountain Show runs every thirty minutes after sunset. Dancing water, dramatic music, and the Burj Khalifa lighting up the sky behind it. It's completely free but feels like a front row seat to something unforgettable. These spots are less crowded, more relaxed and just as photogenic as the paid ones. Sometimes more. Now those views may be free, but roaming charges aren't. Let's talk about how to stay connected without spending a fortune. Let's start with what not to waste money on. First, tap water is safe to drink. Thanks to desalination, it's thoroughly tested and clean, though the taste might not be for everyone. That's why most people still stick to bottled water, especially in hotels and restaurants. As for WiFi, you'll find it almost everywhere from hotels and cafes to malls and even the metro. And the speeds? Surprisingly solid. If you're in Dubai for more than a couple of days, grab a local SIM card at the airport. For around $10 you'll get way more value than using international roaming. Just look for Etisalat or DU kiosks when you land. No need for pricey tourist hotspots or rental routers. Now that you're digitally connected, let's talk about getting around the city. Getting around Dubai is easier than most people expect, but only if you know which option works best. The Dubai metro is fast, clean and fully air conditioned. It's great for major spots like the airport, Dubai Mall, and Marina. But outside those zones, it doesn't reach everything. That's where taxis and ride apps come in. Official taxis are cream colored with a colored roof. They're metered, reliable, and often cheaper than Uber or Careem, especially during peak hours. So which one should you use? If you're headed somewhere off the metro grid, like the Palm Or Outer Islands or traveling in a group, taxis or Careem are your best bet. For longer solo trips, the metro usually wins. Avoid unlicensed cabs and always check the meter. And speaking of getting around, the real challenge in Dubai isn't traffic, it's the heat. You think you've felt heat before? Dubai redefines it. From May to September, daytime temperatures can hit 45 degrees Celsius. And even the shade offers little relief. It's not just uncomfortable, it can be dangerous if you're not careful. You'll see almost no one walking outside midday. Even short strolls can lead to exhaustion or heat stroke. Use the metro, stay hydrated, and take AC breaks. Always carry water, even short outings can turn brutal fast. Dress light, use sunscreen and don't assume your body will adjust in a day or two, it won't. Tourists often pack their days full, but in this kind of heat less is more. The desert heat is no joke, but beyond the temperatures, most travelers wonder, is Dubai actually safe? Dubai is one of the safest cities in the world, Not just in the region but globally. Crime is extremely low. You'll see people leave phones or bags on cafe tables and no one touches them. Violent crime is rare. And strict laws mean even petty theft is taken seriously. But safety here goes beyond crime. If you're a part of a marginalized group, especially LGBTQ plus or outspoken on politics, there are legal gray areas. Dubai is tolerant but not completely open. That said solo travelers including women report feeling safer in Dubai than in many Western cities, even late at night. Respect local customs, avoid confrontations and you'll likely feel safer here than almost anywhere else. Feeling safe is one thing, but being comfortable while you explore. That depends entirely on when you visit. Timing matters. And visiting Dubai at the wrong time can ruin the experience. Peak summer, June through August isn't just hot, it's punishing. Outdoor attractions are nearly empty because no one wants to be out there. While hotel prices drop, you'll find tours canceled, events on pause, and even some beach areas deserted by midday. Also be mindful of public holidays like Eid or National Day. These bring massive crowds, high prices and limited access to attractions. And while Ramadan is a culturally rich time to visit, it comes with restrictions. Shorter business hours, quiet public spaces and no public eating or drinking during daylight. If you are visiting for photography or outdoor adventure, avoid the hottest months and big holidays. The best time, November to March. Mild weather, outdoor events and vibrant city life. And once you've picked your timing, you'll wanna make sure you understand Dubai's unique weekly rhythm. Here's a fun twist. In Dubai, the weekend isn't Saturday and Sunday. Since 2022, the UAE has followed a Monday to Friday work week, making Saturday and Sunday the official weekend. But old habits die hard. Friday is still spiritually significant with midday prayer services and many businesses opening late. Mall stay packed, brunches get booked, and traffic spikes. Tourists often plan outings early Friday morning only to find late openings or unexpected crowds. Want fewer crowds? Hit major attractions mid week, like Tuesday or Wednesday. You'll save time and sometimes even money. Dubai has a rhythm of its own and syncing with it makes your visit smoother. But there's another local rule that trips up tourists. One that comes in a bottle. Yes, you can drink in Dubai, but no. You can't drink anywhere you want. Alcohol is legal, but only in licensed venues like hotel bars, upscale restaurants and specific beach clubs. You won't find it in supermarkets or local convenience stores. Public drunkenness? Illegal. Drinking on the beach? Also illegal. Carrying a bottle around? That can get you fined or worse detained. The safest way to enjoy a drink? Inside your hotel bar or a licensed lounge. No issues, no stress. Also, never drink and drive. Zero tolerance. One sip can cost you thousands or worse. Dubai isn't dry, but it is very regulated. Now let's shift gears from rules and fines to something totally immersive. A Dubai experience you absolutely shouldn't skip. Everyone does a desert safari, but not everyone does it right. Some companies offer evening tours with barbecues, belly dancing and loud crowds. Fun? Maybe. But it's more tourist trap than magic. Instead go for a sunrise desert safari or private experience. You'll ride camels, see golden dunes at first light and escape the noise completely. Want to splurge? Try desert glamping at places like Al Marmoom. Luxury tents, private dining and stars overhead. Just avoid ultra cheap tours with long pick up times, low quality food, or rushed experiences. And skip the quad bikes unless you're comfortable in deep sand, but they're not for beginners. But your final memory of Dubai might not come from a tour or a skyline. It might come from the quietest place you visit. Here's what no guidebook will tell you. Dubai isn't just about what you see. It's about how you connect. Most visitors bounce from malls to beaches to towers, never stopping to explore the city's soul. But if you take a walk through Alpha Hyde Historical District, sip coffee at a local cafe or visit a mosque that welcomes questions, You'll realize something deeper is happening here. Dubai isn't just modern, it's layered. It has roots, stories and neighborhoods that reward the curious. Talk to people. Ask questions. Get lost in the old souks. Visit a neighborhood restaurant instead of the next five star brunch. Those moments quiet, unexpected, and real might just be the ones you remember most. So now you know what to do, what to avoid, and what to expect. Dubai isn't just about the skyscrapers. It's about knowing how to move through the city like a traveler, not a tourist. If this video helped you plan smarter, check out the one we've linked next. It covers the latest travel rule changes that could impact your next trip. And if you're heading to Dubai in 2025, enjoy the journey. You're going to love it.

videos.transcript: They call it the Manhattan of the Middle East. But Dubai isn't just a city. It's a world of its own. Luxury one moment, desert wilderness the next. But here's the problem. Most people come here for a few days and end up overwhelmed by endless choices, overpriced experiences, and way too much time wasted figuring out what's actually worth doing. If you've got just 5 days in Dubai, you need a plan that helps you see the most breathtaking sites, experience the magic of the desert, and still have time to relax and soak it all in. I'm Annie, and in this video, I'm giving you my complete 5-day Dubai itinerary that shows you exactly where to go, what to do, and how to do it. All curated for 2025. From luxury shopping and ultramodern buildings to desert adventures and cultural gems that most people miss. If Dubai has been on your list, this is the only guide you need. So, let's dive right in and start off with five essential things you're definitely going to need to know about before you get here. Dubai is one of the UAE's seven emirates, and with summer temperatures exceeding 50°, you're going to want to plan your visit between November and March for the most pleasant weather. Dubai stretches along the coastline of the Persian Gulf, and it's a fairly spread out city. Some of the key areas we're going to visit in this video are downtown Dubai, where you'll find the Burj Khalifa and Dubai Mall, Jira Beach, Oldtown Dubai, the man-made island of Pong Jira, and the Dubai Marina area. There's plenty of accommodation to suit all budgets in all of these areas. But the city isn't the most walkable, especially in the heat, so you'll want to make use of the metro to get around, especially between downtown and areas furthest away like Pongjra or the marina. UAE's currency is the D room or AED. And with Dubai becoming increasingly cashless, you're going to need a credit card or a multicurrency debit card for your trip like Wise or Revolute. And one last tip, download the Kareem app before you go. It's Dubai's everything app and is great for booking taxi rides or even daily discounts on food, shopping, and other things. Now we've got all that out of the way, let's dive into our first day where we're going to explore the iconic landmarks, worldclass shopping, and beautiful prominards that make downtown Dubai a must visit for any visitor. This is where you'll find the towering Burj Khalifa skyscraper, which at 828 m high, is the tallest building in the world and one of Dubai's must-see sites. It looks like it's about to blast off into space, especially if you come here when it's lit up at night. And at the size of three Eiffel Towers or two Empire State buildings, it's pretty difficult to miss. It cost a whopping $1.5 billion to build. And there's an incredible 160 floors. You can ride to the main observation decks on floors 124 and 125, or go all the way up to the highest outdoor observation deck in the world on the 154th floor. The views from up here are obviously incredible. And being this high, you get a real sense of the landscape of Dubai and how the city melts into the desert. At around 180 dirham each, it's a little pricey, but one way you can save some money is with one of the explorer passes, which give discounted entry for up to seven different attractions, including the Burge. And make sure to come back here in the evening to see the lights and the water show. And if you want to visit the inside, it's one of the most popular places in Dubai to visit. So definitely something best to book up for in advance. Everything about Dubai is the biggest, best, and world record-breaking. And right next to the Burj Khalifa is where you'll find the world's largest and craziest shopping mall, the Dubai Mall. It's an amazing luxury shopping experience with more than 1,200 stores and over 200 restaurants. But there's loads more in here, too. not only an Olympics-sized ice skating rink, but also this amazing fountain, which I just love, and even a 150 million-year-old dinosaur, which was discovered in Wyoming. And one of the best places to check out while you're here is the enormous Dubai Aquarium and underwater zoo, which is full of sharks, rays, and all kinds of fish. And there's even a tunnel you can walk through to see them swimming above. Who puts an aquarium in a shopping mall? If you're looking for somewhere a bit more authentic to shop, then cross the short bridge from the mall to Sucral Baha, an Arabian style market full of Middle Eastern crafts, jewelry, perfumes, and souvenir. The architecture adds a wonderful backdrop for photos, and it's a spot I always recommend for a unique glimpse of local design in downtown. Now, this next building has to be one of the most awinspiring buildings I've ever visited, and the Museum of the Future definitely lives up to its nickname of the most beautiful building on Earth. The symbolism behind it makes it even more impressive. The building's circular form represents humanity. The green mound it sits on represents the earth. And the void in the middle represents our unknown future. Wrapping the outside are three quotes in Arabic from Shik Muhammad bin Rashid al-Maktum emphasizing the future isn't something to wait for but to be created. It's full of futuristic ideas for Dubai. And inside, it's like stepping into the space age with spiral staircases, futuristic elevators, and a ceiling that glows with more Arabic quotes. This is another place you're definitely going to need to book in advance. And a great tip is to visit at sunset because when it gets dark, the entire structure glows in the night with the city shining behind it. Evening in Dubai is just magical. And we're going to end our first day at the spectacular Dubai Fountain, the world's largest water show. A must-see for its incredible water and light display with the Burj Khalifa as the backdrop. It was designed by the same company that created the Bellagio fountains in Las Vegas and projectors and more than 6,000 lights bring the five circles and two arches to life for a display every 30 minutes. You can also stick around after for a separate Burj Khalifa light show which is just as amazing. This is definitely an awesome thing to do after dark. And a top tip, for a few dollars, you can go out on the Dubai Fountain Boardwalk and escape the crowds to get right up close to the show. It's definitely worth the small cost. There's still plenty more amazing places coming up to show you. But if you're dreaming about all the places in the world you want to see, and enjoy watching videos like this, this, and this, then why not subscribe and leave me a comment about your dream travel destinations. After Dubai's modern marvels, Day 2 is all about exploring the traditions, culture, and history of this amazing city that just 60 years ago wasn't much more than a simple fishing port. The best places to experience Dubai's past are in Ber Dubai, the oldest neighborhood in the city, the Alahhidedi historic neighborhood, and Alif. These neighborhoods trace the city's evolution from a small fishing port to the modern marvel it is today. Start your day with a relaxed stroll along Alc's waterfront. This picturesque place is filled with artisal shops, local crafts, and cultural exhibits. Next, check out the historic Alfahidi district, also called Vastakia. Persian merchants established it in the late 1800s, making it one of the oldest parts of the city. Its narrow alleys, wind towers, and mudbrick homes give a sense of Dubai before the skyscrapers. Also worth a visit is the Dubai Museum and Cultural Center. Set inside the 200-year-old Alfahidi Fort, it's a great place to learn all about Dubai's history. Another must-do activity in Ber Dubai is taking an Abra across the creek to Derra. You can catch these traditional wooden boats at a few different points along the creek side and the fair is only 1 AED per person. While you're here, head over to the Dyra Grand Souk and the Gold Souk across from Dubai Creek. The spice souk is also an absolute must. Now, one place you really have to visit to understand the connection between the past and the present in this rapidly changing city is the Dubai frame. To be completely honest, at first I thought the concept of a giant goldplated picture frame in the middle of the city was a little strange. But when I got to the top, I realized how it's positioned so one side looks over old Dubai where everything began. And to the other side is a panorama of one of the world's most futuristic cities. There's also really cool see-through floors which change when you walk on them and give you a glimpse of the ground 150 m below. The good thing is once you're in, you're not on a time limit, so you can stay up there as long as you want. But one thing to know is the cues can be really long to get in. However, the bonus of this attraction is at 50 dirham each. It's much cheaper than the Burj Khalifa and still with incredible views. So definitely one to consider if your budget is limited. For our second evening, we're going to head across the city to check out the Dubai Miracle Garden or the Global Village, or maybe both. There's endless fun things to see in the Dubai Miracle Garden with famous buildings and structures completely recreated with over 50 million flowers. Exhibits include a Smurf village, a flower princess floating above her castle, and a life-sized floral replica of an Emirates airplane, which is the world's largest floral display. And if you still have some spare time, why not check out Global Village as well? It's open pretty late and is a huge outdoor theme park where you can take a world tour without leaving Dubai. It brings together pavilions from over 70 countries, each showcasing their unique culture, food, crafts, and performances. You'll find everything from shopping stalls offering traditional goods, live performances, street entertainers, and cultural shows. Dubai is probably one of the most well-rounded cities in the world with everything from record-breaking buildings to amazing theme parks and beautiful deserts. But to have all this and be right next to the pristine beaches of the Persian Gulf, you really can't beat it. There are three man-made islands off the coast of Dubai, but the biggest and most famous is the Palm JRA. This palm treeshaped island stretches 5 km into the Arabian Gulf, cost 12 billion, and took 6 years to build. And it's so massive that you can apparently see it from space. It's a mustsea for any firsttime visitor. Visit the view at the Palm for stunning 360° panoramic views of Palm Jira, the Arabian Gulf, and the Dubai skyline from the 52nd floor of the Palm Tower. But my favorite experience by far was to visit the Aura Sky Pool and Lounge, which is home to the world's highest infinity pool, more than 200 m above the ground. The pool stretches 360° around the hotel and gives you just the most incredible views of the Palm and the city while you relax with a cocktail on the sun lounges. But this books up really fast. So definitely one you're going to want to book way in advance. So I've left a link for you in the description below. Or if you prefer something more thrilling, how about taking a helicopter tour? Or if you're an adrenaline junkie, even a skydive. I don't know of many other places you can skydive with a view like this. At the far end of the Palm, you've got the incredible Palm Atlantis Hotel. You can stay here, and you can also have a full day of fun at the Aqua Venture Water Park, which holds the world record for the most water slides. This is definitely a great spot to check out if you have kids, and you'll definitely want to head to the Tower of Neptune for the Leap of Faith, Atlantis's most famous water slide. Another great attraction in the Palm Atlantis is the Lost Chambers Aquarium, a stunning underwater world filled with over 65,000 marine creatures. There are plenty of beaches lining the coast. One of the most popular is JBR Beach, which you're going to find right near the marina area. Even though it can get pretty crowded, it's a great place to chill out by the water and the best spot to take in the sunset. And there's loads of activities here, a huge inflatable pontoon, all kinds of water sports going on, and even the largest ferris wheel in the world. The beach is lined with hotels, and you also get a great view of the Palm Atlantis from here as well. Now, one of my favorite parts of Dubai to stay is the marina area. You can find some really high-end apartments here on Airbnb for around $1 to $150 a night. It's close to the JBR beach, not far from the Palm and about a 25-minute metro ride to downtown. You're going to find plenty of yachts here, plus restaurants and shopping, all surrounded by super modern high-rise buildings that reflect the water in their windows and look magical at night when they're lit up. It reminds me a bit of Sydney's Darling Harbor, and it's a great place to take a boat tour from or hang out in the evening for great food, bars, cocktails, and nightife. It's our fourth day, and we're going to spend it taking a day trip to neighboring Abu Dhabi. And even though it's not in Dubai, in my opinion, it's a musto while you're here. Abu Dhabi is the largest of the United Arab Emirates and also the capital. It takes about an hour and a half by car or bus from Dubai to Abu Dhabi. Or there are plenty of organized tours you can take. The first place you're going to want to visit is the Louvre Museum. Yes, named just like the original one in France. In fact, the UAE paid France $400 million just for the right to use the name. There's plenty of cool art to see, but the real thing to be excited about here is the spectacular architecture. A huge floating dome on top of an all-white building contrasts perfectly with the surrounding blue water. What's really cool about the dome is its eight different layers that create a rain of light effect coming through as you're walking around. Another of the main reasons to visit Abu Dhabi is for the Shake Zed Grand Mosque, which is the ninth largest mosque in the world and can fit over 40,000 worshippers inside at once. It's one of the most spectacular buildings I've ever seen. Maybe, just maybe, as impressive as India's Tarsh Mahal, especially the expanse of all white marble. But keep in mind, this is a place of worship and there's a dress code here where men have to cover their shoulders and knees and women have to cover pretty much everything apart from their face. After you can visit some of the palaces like the Emirates Palace, which is now turned into a hotel, or check out the presidential palace where all the government meetings are held. And if you fancy another theme park, then why not check out Ferrari World, the world's first Ferrari themed park and home of Formula Rossa, the world's fastest roller coaster. There's still absolutely tons to see and do in Dubai. So, for our last day, I'm going to give you some options you can choose from. Everything from a desert safari to spending the day at one of Dubai's many theme parks or even literally diving into another of Dubai's record-breaking attractions. And if you're interested in visiting any of the places I've talked about in this video or experiences I'm about to show you, you can book any of these through the VOR app. Vitor is a platform with thousands of experiences worldwide where you can book tours, excursions, entrance tickets, and even skip the line. Opportunities just like the Burj Khalifa. I've got to skip the entire massive line there. And they also let you cancel 24 hours before your tour starts, and you get all your money back if you change your mind. I'll link everything for you in the description below. For me, taking a desert safari is one of the things everyone should experience when you're visiting Dubai. There's plenty of organized tours to choose from to suit your budget and preferences. I chose this one, which included activities from dune bashing in an all-terrain vehicle to a quad biking tour, sandboarding and camel riding, a barbecue and evening entertainment at a Bedawin camp, and even stargazing. The tour began with a pickup from the hotel and a scenic 1-hour drive out into the Dubai desert. Our first activity was quad biking in the open desert dunes, which was great fun, but also done with a guide, so it's really safe. After that, we jumped in a 4x4 for a real roller coaster adventure over some of the highest dunes. It was an absolute blast. Then, we headed across the desert to Majilus Bedwin Safari Camp, which celebrates the culture and traditions of these nomadic Arabian tribes. There's no roads to get there, so you have to use 4x4 jeeps to cross the desert. Here, there were activities like camel riding and sandboarding, an amazing barbecue dinner, and finished off with traditional dancing and a fire show. For thrillsekers, there's no shortage of worldclass theme parks in Dubai to fuel your adrenaline. A few of the top ones include the movie themed parks at IMG Worlds of Adventure, where you can ride the Velociraptor, one of the tallest and fastest roller coasters in Dubai, and Motion Gate Dubai with rides inspired by action films including Ghostbusters and The Hunger Games. Inside the Dubai Mall, you'll find Play DXB, the largest indoor virtual reality park in the world, where you can try the Burge Drop, a seriously nailbiting virtual reality experience that sees you hanging onto the Burj Khalifa by a thread. Or check out Real Madrid World, one of the newest attractions in Dubai and the world's first football theme park, which includes the world's tallest amusement ride at 460 ft. Another great experience you can try in Dubai is a sunrise hot air balloon ride over the desert. The mornings provide perfect weather conditions, and as you float between 2,000 and 4,000 ft above the protected Dubai Desert Conservation Reserve, you'll get the most amazing views of the sunrise along with expert commentary from your pilot about the geology and wildlife of the area. There's no shortage of record-breaking attractions in Dubai. And another one of these is Deep Dive Dubai. A stunning underwater attraction inspired by the UAE's own pearl diving heritage. Themed to resemble an abandoned sunken city, this extraordinary indoor pool is filled with 14 million L of fresh water and lets divers submerged to depths of up to 60 m. You can explore all kinds of sunken rooms and themed areas. And there's options available for both certified and non-certified divers, as well as snorkeling and free diving. Full disclosure though, it's fairly pricey and another one of the experiences you're going to want to book plenty of time in advance because it's really popular. They say that whatever you're looking for, you can find it in Dubai. But if you're planning a trip here, there's definitely some things you're going to need to know about before you visit. So, make sure to check out this video here next. With that said, thanks for watching. Don't forget to like, subscribe, and leave me a comment about your favorite things to do in Dubai. And I'll see you next time.

videos.transcript: Welcome to Dubai, one of the busiest airports in the world and the city of glamour, glitz, and luxury. Dubai is a cosmopolitan hub on the Arabian Peninsula that will take your breath away with its towering skyscrapers, exotic experiences, and rich cultural diversity. As a first-time visitor, you'll have the opportunity to immerse yourself in a futuristic Arabian fairy tale with plenty of sights to see and places to visit in Dubai. However, while Dubai is a more Westernized city in the Middle East, there are still rigorous regulations and norms to be aware of, especially when it comes to the dress code. Despite this, Dubai is one of the safest countries in the world with plenty of budget-friendly hotels and fantastic dining alternatives. Welcome to the Itinerant Traveler, and in this video, we'll be going over the eight sights to see and places to visit if you're in Dubai for the first time. If you enjoy our video, be sure to like and subscribe to stay up-to-date with our future videos. Number eight, Ski Dubai. In the UAE, Dubai is home to Ski Dubai, a world-famous indoor ski resort housed within the Mall of the Emirates, which we'll go into later in this video. It's one of the world's largest indoor ski resorts with a total area of 22,500 square meters. Among its amenities are a snow park, equipment rentals, and five slopes of varying difficulty. Ski Dubai keeps its indoor temperature between -1 and 2° C all year round, making it ideal for skiing and snowboarding. Snow tubing, tobogganing, and a penguin encounter are just a few of the other snow-related activities available to guests here. There's also a ski rental shop, a cafe, and a viewing gallery at the resort. Many people visit because it provides the unusual opportunity to engage in winter sports in the middle of a desert, making it an appealing tourist destination. Number seven, Dubai Miracle Garden. Escape to a world of color and beauty at the Dubai Miracle Garden, a 72,000 square meter oasis that all visitors have to add on their list of places to visit in Dubai. Over 50 million flowers are arranged into stunning displays that change with the seasons, creating a unique and exciting experience each time you visit. Stroll through the flower-lined heart-shaped paths, enjoy the elaborate floral sculptures and decorations, and be amazed by the world's largest indoor butterfly garden, where you can wander among thousands of free-flying butterflies in a temperature-controlled setting. The Dubai Miracle Garden, which is open from October to April, provides a tranquil retreat for nature lovers and Instagram aficionados alike. Complete with restaurants, cafes, and gift stores to make for a fantastic day out with friends and family. Number six, Dubai Fountain. Located in the heart of Dubai, the Dubai Fountain is a massive choreographed fountain system. It's one of the largest and can be found in Burj Khalifa's lake in front of the world's tallest structure, the Burj Khalifa, which we'll discuss later in the video. The fountain can spray water up to 152 m, 500 ft into the air, and is illuminated by more than 6,000 lights and 25 color projectors. The fountain plays music from many genres, such as classical pieces, Arabic songs, and modern hits, and the water dances in time with the music. There are multiple daily performances of the fountain, and they're open to the public at no cost. This makes it one of the most affordable sights to see in Dubai. It opened to the public in 2009 after being designed by WET Design, the same firm that made the fountains at the Bellagio Hotel in Las Vegas. Number five, Dubai Mall and the Mall of the Emirates. The Dubai Mall and the Mall of the Emirates are two of the world's largest and most well-known retail centers, making Dubai a shopper's wonderland. Over 1,200 shops, 200 restaurants and cafes, an Olympic-sized ice rink, an indoor aquarium, a children's edutainment center, and much more can be found within the Dubai Mall's 12 million square feet of space. The Mall of the Emirates, on the other hand, is a paradise for shoppers since it features over 630 shops and 100 restaurants, and is a one-stop shop for all shopping needs, from designer clothes to authentic Arabian trinkets. Due to the high temperatures, tourists are advised to avoid going out during the day and instead spend their time shopping and waiting for the evening to see the city's nightlife. If you're looking for a memorable shopping experience in Dubai, make sure to stop by both the Dubai Mall and the Mall of the Emirates. Number four, Dubai Aquarium and Underwater Zoo. Located in the Dubai Mall, the Dubai Aquarium and Underwater Zoo is a popular destination for locals and visitors alike. The incredible diversity of marine life on show will astound guests of this aquarium, which boasts a 10 million-liter tank home to more than 33,000 aquatic animals, including the world's largest collection of sand tiger sharks. The glass-bottom excursions and cage snorkeling experiences brings visitors up close and personal with the aquatic life, while the 48-meter underwater viewing tunnel gives them a unique viewpoint on the marine world. Also noteworthy is the zoo, which is home to several species of animals ranging from reptiles and birds to mammals and amphibians.
